one_line_takeaway: MSB is the first execution-based benchmark showing MCP-specific tool attacks are readily exploitable across all pipeline stages (40.35% avg ASR, 76.5% for out-of-scope parameters), and that more capable agents are paradoxically more vulnerable while current detection defenses barely move overall resilience.
key_finding: All 12 attack types are effective, with an overall average ASR of 40.35% across models. The Out-of-Scope Parameter (OP) attack at the invocation stage is by far the most damaging (avg ASR 76.5%; invocation stage >70% ASR overall), showing the tool-parameter interface is the weakest link. MCP-specific response attacks outperform classic function-calling attacks: the authors' User Impersonation (45.69%) and False Error (39.21%) beat Prompt Injection (20.21%) and Retrieval Injection (20%). Mixed attacks are synergistic (e.g., PI-UI 56.07% > both PI and UI alone). A clear inverse-scaling relationship holds: more capable models are MORE vulnerable because their stronger tool-use and instruction-following abilities make them better at executing the attacker's tool calls — DeepSeek-V3.1 has both the highest ASR (60.94%) and highest PUA. NRP disentangles this trade-off: GPT-5 achieves the highest NRP (best utility with moderate resistance). The MCIP defense only marginally helps overall resilience (average NRP +1.18%) while lowering ASR by 11.66% at the cost of 7.59% utility (over-rejection/functional degradation).
core_contribution: MSB (MCP Security Bench) is an end-to-end benchmark for measuring how well LLM agents resist attacks specific to the Model Context Protocol (MCP) across the full tool-use pipeline (task planning, tool invocation, response handling). Its own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of 12 attack types organized by MCP interaction vector and stage — Tool Signature attacks (Name Collision, Preference Manipulation, Prompt Injection), a Tool Parameter attack (Out-of-Scope Parameter), Tool Response attacks (User Impersonation, False Error, Tool Transfer), Retrieval Injection, and multi-stage Mixed attacks; (2) a dynamic evaluation harness that executes attacks by actually running real benign and malicious tools over MCP rather than simulating tool outputs; and (3) a new robustness metric, Net Resilient Performance (NRP = PUA*(1-ASR)), that jointly captures utility and security in adversarial settings. The paper positions User Impersonation (a tool response that pretends to be the user issuing a new urgent instruction) as its own newly-introduced attack. Note: benchmarks like ASB, AgentDojo, InjecAgent, and MCPTox are discussed as prior/related work the paper differentiates from, not as its own contribution.
threat_model: The attacker controls a malicious MCP server and all tools hosted on it, and can publish these tools to third-party registries (e.g., Smithery) so an agent adds them to its tool list. The attacker can modify every component of a malicious tool (name, description, parameters, response), deploy multiple coordinated malicious tools on one server, and — via MCP's tool-discovery mechanism — insert malicious content into the agent's system prompt. The attacker also has white-box access to external resources and can plant covert instructions in data the agent later retrieves (poisoned database Dp). Crucially, the attacker does NOT control the LLM itself and CANNOT intercept or directly edit the user's query (a deliberately weaker/more realistic assumption than prior work such as ASB). The attack goal is to steer the agent's decisions in planning, tool-calling, or response handling into performing a malicious action a_m (e.g., leaking sensitive data, modifying stored data, killing a process, writing an SSH key to an attacker path).
limitations: Attacks are confined to file/workspace-style objectives via FileSystem and DesktopCommander servers, so real-world harm diversity is limited to the chosen 6 attack tasks and 10 scenarios. There is a stated count inconsistency in the paper itself (abstract/intro variously say 'ten popular LLM agents,' '9 popular LLM backbones,' and 'ten popular LLM agents,' while Table 3 reports 10 models). The paper reports GPT-5 near-immune to UI/FE (0.33%/1.31% ASR) yet still highly exploitable via OP (98.75%), so per-model conclusions are attack-specific and don't generalize. Only one defense (MCIP, a static detection classifier) is evaluated, and the authors acknowledge it over-rejects and cannot handle response-based attacks where the tool schema stays legitimate until after invocation; no adaptive/context-aware defense is proposed or tested. The '75.83% peak ASR' cited in the intro is not clearly tied to a specific cell (Table 3 shows several higher single-cell values, e.g., 98.75%).

datasets_used: MSB benchmark (authors' own): 2,000 attack test instances across 10 scenarios, 65 user tasks, 25 MCP servers, 304 benign tools, 405 attack tools, 6 attack tasks, 12 attack types
Benign tools sourced from the Smithery MCP registry (most-used tools, after functionality verification and deduplication)
Runtime environment MCP servers: FileSystem and DesktopCommander (file access + workspace manipulation), plus target sensitive data and poisoned external resource files
quantitative_results: Overall average ASR across all attacks and models: 40.35%
Highest-ASR attack: Out-of-Scope Parameter (OP) avg 76.5%; lowest: NC-FE avg 14.62%
MCP-specific response attacks: User Impersonation avg 45.69%, False Error avg 39.21%, vs Prompt Injection 20.21% and Retrieval Injection 20%
Mixed-attack synergy: PI-UI avg 56.07%, PI-FE 52.86%, TT-OP 53.75%, PM-OP 42.50%
Invocation stage least secure (>70% avg ASR)
Most vulnerable model overall: DeepSeek-V3.1 (avg ASR 60.94%, highest PUA); GPT-5 highest NRP; Claude 4 Sonnet avg ASR 52.51%; GPT-4o-mini 58.56%
baselines_compared: No-defense baseline vs MCIP detection defense (Jing et al. 2025; Llama-xLAM-2-8B risk classifier) - the paper's only experimental defense comparison
10 LLM backbones evaluated as subjects: DeepSeek-V3.1, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5, Claude 4 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Qwen3 8B, Qwen3 30B, Llama3.1 8B, Llama3.1 70B, Llama3.3 70B
Prior benchmarks contrasted qualitatively in related work (NOT run as baselines): ASB, AgentDojo, InjecAgent, MCPTox
